audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You'll hear two teachers discussing arrangements for a goodbye party for a colleague.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First you have some time to look at questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You'll see that there is an example that has been done for you, on this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Tony.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Thanks ever so much for coming.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: You know we've been asked to organize something for John's farewell.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Yeah, sure.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Uh. It's about time we started working out details.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: We don't want to leave it so late that it's double the work.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Hmm, right.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Uh. Do you want me to take notes?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Oh, that would be great, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Right, first thing is when is the best time to hold it?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Well, he leaves on the 24th of December.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: So what about the 22nd?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Yeah, I think that's about right.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: We want it quite near the time, don't we?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: The date of the goodbye party is the 22nd of December.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So 22nd December has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Tony.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Thanks ever so much for coming.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: You know we've been asked to organize something for John's farewell.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Yeah, sure.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Uh. It's about time we started working out details.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: We don't want to leave it so late that it's double the work.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Hmm, right.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Uh. Do you want me to take notes?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Oh, that would be great, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Right, first thing is when is the best time to hold it?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Well, he leaves on the 24th of December.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: So what about the 22nd?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Yeah, I think that's about right.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: We want it quite near the time, don't we?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Sure, uh, and what about a venue?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: In college, uh, a hotel?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: I think a hotel will probably work out rather expensive.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: And I've been looking at the college dining room.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: That seems pretty reasonable.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Fine.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Yeah, why not?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: And then we ought to be thinking about invitations.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Um. Who mustn't we forget to invite?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Well, well obviously John and his wife.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Oh, right.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: And the director.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: The office staff.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Yep.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: And all the teachers and all the students.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Uh, anyone else?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Uh. Faculty heads?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Hmm. No, better draw the line.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: I don't think it's necessary.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Yeah, you're right.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: I don't mind writing the invitations.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Uh. When shall we get them out for?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Hmm. Enough time, but not too early.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Uh. What about the 15th of December?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Well, there are exams on the 16th, better avoid them.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Hmm. 10th.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Yeah, that should do it.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, what does that leave?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Oh, yes, um, a present.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Um. Would you mind doing that?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: No, not at all.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: We usually go around with an envelope during coffee break, don't we?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Yeah, coffee breaks always the best time, cause people have got their money handy.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Yeah, exactly.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Uh. Do we suggest an amount or does it seem a bit unfair?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: No, I think people welcome it.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: We suggested $6 last time.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Is that OK?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Yeah, plenty I would have thought.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Which should leave us with about $90, have you any ideas for presents?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Well, I've been having a little think, I thought, well, you know, um, he loves music.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Hmm. Yeah, and books.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: So, I thought I'd check on prices for well, uh, perhaps CD players.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Hmm. Yeah, that's that's a good idea.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: And also I thought maybe you know a set of dictionaries.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: I heard him say he needed a good one.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: The other thing he was saying last week was that his computer printer had broken.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Um. No, uh, I'd be really frightened about getting the wrong type.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: OK, yeah.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: The other thing is something for the home.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Uh. Jill suggested a coffee maker.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Oh, yeah.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: I'll certainly find out what they cost.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Uh. OK.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Have you got all that down?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Uh. Yes.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Now we need to think a little more about the money.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: I know we've got a set amount from the social fund.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Hmm, right.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Uh. What does that cover?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: It's meant to cover the cost of the room.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: And a certain amount for food.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: And also drinks?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Oh, yeah, certainly.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: But will it be enough?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: What we've done in the past is to ask guests to bring some snacks.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: We don't ask them to bring more drinks, because we figure that's well, that should come from the social fund.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: OK, uh, anything else for the guests to bring?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Well, uh, oh, some music.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Because there'll be a tape deck there in the room, and we can have some dancing later on.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Uh. Anything else?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Well, it's just a thought, but a couple of years ago we had a really good party where we set up, you know, some simple games.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Yeah, great.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Uh. Wasn't it based on photos from the students and teachers?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Uh. So we should ask the guests to bring photos.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: OK, I'll put it on the invitations.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Now the last thing is who shall we ask to do the speech?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Hmm. Don't you think it might be nice to have one of the students?
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Well, uh, then the student leader.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Yeah, much better than the director giving speeches again.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: OK, then I'll ask her.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Hmm. Lovely.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: So, is that all?
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: It looks like it.
audioscript.speaker: TONY

audioscript.text: Great.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: Thanks ever so much.
audioscript.speaker: LISA

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers. Now turn to section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER

items: Ask student representative to prepare a <input>
body.title: GOODBYE PARTY FOR JOHN

answer_key.correct_answer: set of dictionaries | dictionaries | a good dictionary
music | some music | music tapes | some music tapes | tapes | some tapes

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You'll hear a pre recorded message on the Travelite Travel Agency Information Line.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Thank you for calling the free Travelite Travel Agency Information Line.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 1

audioscript.text: You will not be charged for this call.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 1

audioscript.text: In order to deal with all calls effectively, we offer you a number of options.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 1

audioscript.text: Please listen carefully and press your required number at the appropriate time, or dial a new number.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 1

audioscript.text: If you want to hear about special offers, please press 1.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 1

audioscript.text: If you want to hear our latest price lists, please press 2.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 1

audioscript.text: If you want to make a complaint, please press 3.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 1

audioscript.text: If you want information about our new walking holidays, please press 4 now.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 1

audioscript.text: Thank you for calling our Travelite Walking Holidays Line.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: We have been offering a wide variety of walking holidays to suit all tastes for just three years, but already we have won two awards for excellence in this field.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: We offer guided walking tours to suit the discerning traveler in 12 different centers throughout the whole of Western Europe.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: We are planning to open our first center outside this area in the coming year, so watch out for developments.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: But the pride of Travelite is the level of guidance and support we offer on our walks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: All are planned in detail by our highly trained guides, who all work in a variety of different Travelite locations, so we can guarantee standards.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: Each day we offer 3 separate walks catering for all skills and fitness levels.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: We also pride ourselves on our friendly service, particularly important for the increasing numbers of people who choose to holiday alone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: Unlike almost all travel operators who happily charge large supplements for single rooms, we guarantee that no single client will pay more, even when only double rooms are available for them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: And the day doesn't end with the return to base.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: After our dinner at communal tables designed to make all our guests feel part of a family atmosphere, entertainment is laid on nearly every night with tour leaders on hand to organize lectures, games, quizzes, and respond to any special requests from guests.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the message, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: The following is a summary of costs and special inclusive offers on holidays for the coming summer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: We have three lengths of holiday: three-day, seven-day and fourteen-day. The three-day holiday costs $180 for all accommodation, food and walking.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: And for the first time this year, we are including in that price the cost of picking you up from the nearest station.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: The seven-day holiday costs $350 per person.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: And as well as including the offers of the three-day holiday, also includes a magnificent book giving the local history.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: On top of that we are able to include free maps.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: For you to better enjoy the walking, and even plan in advance if you wish.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: For the 14 day holiday, our special price is $690 per person.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: And that includes all the offers for the 3 and 7 day holidays, plus membership of a local walking club, so you can better enjoy the full flavor of the local life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: For further information, please contact your local travel agent.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: Thank you for calling the Travelite Travel Agency information line...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER 2

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers. Now turn to Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You'll hear a pre recorded message on the Travelite Travel Agency Information Line.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 15.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Thank you for calling the free Travelite Travel Agency Information Line.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER 1

utterances.text: You will not be charged for this call.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER 1

utterances.text: In order to deal with all calls effectively, we offer you a number of options.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER 1

utterances.text: Please listen carefully and press your required number at the appropriate time, or dial a new number.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER 1

utterances.text: If you want to hear about special offers, please press 1.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER 1

utterances.text: If you want to hear our latest price lists, please press 2.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER 1

utterances.text: If you want to make a complaint, please press 3.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER 1

utterances.text: If you want information about our new walking holidays, please press 4 now.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER 1

utterances.text: Thank you for calling our Travelite Walking Holidays Line.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER 2

utterances.text: We have been offering a wide variety of walking holidays to suit all tastes for just three years, but already we have won two awards for excellence in this field.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER 2

utterances.text: We offer guided walking tours to suit the discerning traveler in 12 different centers throughout the whole of Western Europe.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER 2

utterances.text: We are planning to open our first center outside this area in the coming year, so watch out for developments.

utterances.text: But the pride of Travelite is the level of guidance and support we offer on our walks.

audioscript.text: Section 3, you will hear a conversation between two students who are studying to be teachers talking about science experiments.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Sue.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Hi, Mike.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: So what happened to you last week?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Oh, I was sick with the flu.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: What's this I hear about a big assignment we've got to do?
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Oh, well, basically, we've got to find two science experiments to do with a group of eight year old children at the local primary school.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: And we've got to complete it by the end of the week.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Hmm. That sounds like hard work.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Where are we supposed to get the ideas for these experiments from?
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Well, I managed to get hold of 2 books from the library.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Oh, well done.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: How about if we take a look at the experiments in this book first, and see if anything looks suitable?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: I can make notes as we go about equipment and the purpose of the experiments.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Let's see, um, the first experiment is called 'Make your own hovercraft'.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Ha, which sounds very ambitious.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Ha ha ha.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Mind you, you only need 20 balloons and a table.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: You don't need any special engines or anything like that.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: What do you do with it all?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Uh. You blow up the balloons, and you balance the table on them, upside down, of course, and the kids get to ride around on it.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: You know, the other kids sort of push them around the room.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: The main purpose is to show how hovercrafts work, and how things hover around on just a cushion of air.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: OK, that doesn't sound too bad.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: OK, ready for number two?
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Uh-huh.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Now this one is called 'Unusual Measures of Lengths', and you basically use lots of paper clips.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: The kids go around the class measuring things, you know how long the desk is, and that sort of thing.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Um. And then they all compare their answers.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Uh. And basically, because not all paper clips are the same lengths, they should come up with some strange answers.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: It's supposed to demonstrate the importance of having fixed units of measurement.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Yeah, that's not bad.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: OK, now for No. 3, you need rock salt or copper sulphate.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Oh, I'm not sure about that.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Well, just put down the rock salt then.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Um. Apart from that, you only need a jar of water.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: And basically you dissolve lots of salt into the water, and watch the crystals form.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: So it basically teaches the kids about growing crystals.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Yeah, I suppose it would be nice to grow something.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Hmm, let's move on and have a look at No. 4.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: OK, this one is called 'Spinning Color Wheel'.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: It looks like you get some cardboard and draw a circle on it.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Divide it into 6 equal segments, and color each one in, using different colors.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Then you thread a piece of string through the middle.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: So we'd need some string as well.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Yes, sorry.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Um. And you spin the wheel around, and if you can get it spinning fast enough, hopefully the colors all merge and show up as white.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Oh, I didn't know that.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: What's the principle behind it?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Well it's pretty elementary physics really, it teaches them about how white light or ordinary light is made up.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Hmm, well that doesn't sound too bad.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Now, there's only one more left in this book, isn't there?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: What does that one say?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Um. Well, it's another one where they'd get to make something.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Sounds very interesting.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: You need quite a lot of equipment actually, a hand drill.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: An old record, a pin or needle, some paper, and a bolt.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Hmm, go on.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: What do they have to do?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Well, they basically make a record player.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: The main idea is to teach them about recording sound.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: But hopefully they'd also see that you need motion and an amplifier to make the sound heard.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: OK, well, it does sound interesting.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Shall we go through all of those again and decide if any of them are going to be suitable?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Right, No. 1, I thought this one sounded nice.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: There'll be lots of activity, and it doesn't need too much in the way of equipment.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Yes, that's true, but don't you think it's a bit risky to get a group of 8 year olds pushing each other around a classroom like that?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Well, someone could get hurt.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: No, I don't like the sound of that one at all.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Hmm. Maybe you're right.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: What about No. 2, with the paper clips?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: It sounds tame enough.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Yes, a bit too tame if you ask me.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Hmm. I think it needs to be something a bit more active and interesting than that, don't you?
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Yes, I suppose you're right.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: We won't get a very good mark if the children don't actually enjoy the experiments, and I suppose we could turn them off science for good.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Well, what about the next one, No. 3?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Now, I quite like the idea of this one.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Yeah, so do I.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: But I seem to remember when we did it at high school, we had to wait up to a fortnight before we saw any halfway decent results.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Oh, yes.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Well, that won't be any good then.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: We'll only see the kids for one or two hours at the most.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Yes, and we have to do the experiments and write up our results within a week.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: So that one won't do at all.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: OK, well, what did you think of No. 4?
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: I like the idea of it, but do you think it will be a bit elementary for them?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Well, they are only eight, you know.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: I know, but you know what I mean.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Don't you think the activity itself is a bit babyish?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Hmm. Maybe you're right.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: They might have fun, but I mean cutting out a circle and coloring it in.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: OK, well what about No. 5?
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: I thought this one sounded a bit too good to be true -
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: great equipment.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: But don't you think it's a bit ambitious for this age group?
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: I mean I don't want to start off something and then have to abandon it, if they just can't cope with it.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: I could see us ending up doing just about all of the work for them.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: I guess you're right.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Oh, well, maybe we could store that idea away for later.
audioscript.speaker: MIKE

audioscript.text: Yep, let's hope this second book has something better.
audioscript.speaker: SUE

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers. Now turn to Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Right, No. 1, I thought this one sounded nice.
utterances.speaker: MIKE

utterances.text: There'll be lots of activity, and it doesn't need too much in the way of equipment.
utterances.speaker: MIKE

utterances.text: Yes, that's true, but don't you think it's a bit risky to get a group of 8 year olds pushing each other around a classroom like that?
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: Well, someone could get hurt.
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: No, I don't like the sound of that one at all.
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: Hmm. Maybe you're right.
utterances.speaker: MIKE

utterances.text: What about No. 2, with the paper clips?
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: It sounds tame enough.
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: Yes, a bit too tame if you ask me.
utterances.speaker: MIKE

utterances.text: Hmm. I think it needs to be something a bit more active and interesting than that, don't you?
utterances.speaker: MIKE

utterances.text: Yes, I suppose you're right.
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: We won't get a very good mark if the children don't actually enjoy the experiments, and I suppose we could turn them off science for good.
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: Well, what about the next one, No. 3?
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: Now, I quite like the idea of this one.
utterances.speaker: MIKE

utterances.text: Yeah, so do I.
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: But I seem to remember when we did it at high school, we had to wait up to a fortnight before we saw any halfway decent results.
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: Oh, yes.
utterances.speaker: MIKE

utterances.text: Well, that won't be any good then.
utterances.speaker: MIKE

utterances.text: We'll only see the kids for one or two hours at the most.
utterances.speaker: MIKE

utterances.text: Yes, and we have to do the experiments and write up our results within a week.
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: So that one won't do at all.
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: OK, well, what did you think of No. 4?
utterances.speaker: MIKE

utterances.text: I like the idea of it, but do you think it will be a bit elementary for them?
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: Well, they are only eight, you know.
utterances.speaker: MIKE

utterances.text: I know, but you know what I mean.
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: Don't you think the activity itself is a bit babyish?
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: Hmm. Maybe you're right.
utterances.speaker: MIKE

utterances.text: They might have fun, but I mean cutting out a circle and coloring it in.
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: OK, well what about No. 5?
utterances.speaker: MIKE

utterances.text: I thought this one sounded a bit too good to be true -
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: great equipment.
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: Yeah.
utterances.speaker: MIKE

utterances.text: But don't you think it's a bit ambitious for this age group?
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: I mean I don't want to start off something and then have to abandon it, if they just can't cope with it.
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: I could see us ending up doing just about all of the work for them.
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: I guess you're right.
utterances.speaker: MIKE

utterances.text: Oh, well, maybe we could store that idea away for later.
utterances.speaker: MIKE

utterances.text: Yep, let's hope this second book has something better.
utterances.speaker: SUE

utterances.text: That is the end of section 3.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers. Now turn to Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER

To show why we need standard <input>
To teach children about how <input> is made up
A drill, an old record, a pin / needle, paper, a bolt
To make a record player in order to learn about recording sound

test_text.title: Sharks in Australia: Characteristics and Beach-netting Measures

audioscript.text: Section 4, you'll hear a lecturer talking about the meshing of sharks in Australia.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Today we're going to look at one of my favorite fish, the shark.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As you know, sharks have a reputation for being very dangerous creatures, capable of injuring or killing humans.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And I'd like to talk about sharks in Australia.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Sharks are rather large fish, often growing to over 10 meters, and the longest sharks caught in Australia have reached 16 meters.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Sharks vary in weight with size and breed of course, but the heaviest shark caught in Australia was a white pointer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That weighed 795 kilograms, quite a size.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Sharks have a different structure to most fish.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Instead of a skeleton made of bone, they have a tough elastic skeleton of cartilage.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Unlike bone, this firm pliable material is rather like your nose.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And allows the shark to bend easily as it swims.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The shark's skin isn't covered with scales like other fish.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Instead, the skin's covered with barbs giving it a rough texture like sandpaper.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As you know, sharks are very quick swimmers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is made possible by their fins, one set at the side, and another set underneath the body.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And the tail also helps the shark move forward quickly.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Unlike other fish, sharks have to keep swimming if they want to stay at a particular depth, and they rarely swim at the surface.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Mostly they swim at the bottom of the ocean, scavenging and picking up food that's lying on the ocean floor.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: While most other animals, including fish, hunt their prey by means of their eyesight, sharks hunt essentially by smell.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They have a very acute sense of smell.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And can sense the presence of food long before they can see it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In Australia where people spend a lot of time at the beach, the government has realized that it must prevent sharks from swimming near its beaches.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As a result, they've introduced a beach netting program.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Beach netting or meshing involves setting large nets parallel to the shore.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This means that the nets on New South Wales beaches are set on one day.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And then lifted and taken out to sea on the next day.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When shark netting first began in 1939, only the Sydney metropolitan beaches were meshed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These beaches were chosen because beaches near the city are usually the most crowded with swimmers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Ten years later, in 1949, systematic meshing was extended to include the beaches to the south of Sydney.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As a result of the general success of the program in Sydney, shark meshing was introduced to the state of Queensland around 1970.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The New Zealand authorities also looked at it,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: but considered meshing uneconomical - as did Tahiti in the Pacific.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At around the same time, South Africa introduced meshing to some of its most popular swimming beaches.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When meshing began, approximately 1500 sharks were caught in the first year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, this declined in the years that followed, and since that time, the average annual catch has been only about 150 a year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The majority of sharks are caught during the warmest months from November to February, when sharks are most active, and when both the air and the ocean are at their maximum temperature.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Despite quite large catches, some people believe that shark meshing is not the best way to catch sharks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's not that they think sharks are afraid of nets, or because they eat holes in them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Because neither of these is true, but meshing does appear to be less effective than some other methods, especially when there are big seas with high rolling waves and strong currents.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And anything that lets the sand move, the sand that's holding the nets down, when this moves, the nets will also become less effective.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: That is the end of the listening test.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In the IELTS test, you would now have 10 minutes to transfer your answers to the listening answer sheet.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER
